era_context: The Late Roman Empire under Valentinian I (West, r. 364–375 CE) and Valens (East, r. 364–378 CE), mid-reign. The empire faces sustained military pressure on multiple frontiers: Germanic tribes on the Rhine and Danube, Sasanid Persia in the east, and internal religious division over Arian versus Nicene Christianity. The Council of Nicaea's settlement (325 CE) remains contested, with Arian bishops influential in the Eastern empire and among Germanic peoples.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record only)
East Asia (Chinese sources exist but may be underrepresented)

events.title: Valentinian I campaigns against the Sarmatians on the Danube
events.description: Valentinian I conducts a military campaign against Sarmatian tribes on the Danube frontier. The Sarmatians, an Iranian-speaking nomadic people, have been a persistent threat to Roman control of the Danube region. Valentinian's campaign is part of his broader strategy to stabilize the empire's northern frontiers. The campaign is successful in restoring Roman control and establishing a reputation for military competence, though the frontier remains contested.
key_figures: Sarmatian tribal leaders (names not preserved in sources)
events.certainty_note: Ammianus Marcellinus documents Valentinian's Danube campaigns, though the precise chronology of individual campaigns (368–375) is sometimes compressed or uncertain in the surviving text.
cross_references: Part of Valentinian I's broader frontier stabilization strategy (364–375 CE)
Contemporary with Rhine frontier campaigns (369–370 CE)

events.title: Valens campaigns against the Goths on the Danube
events.description: Valens, Eastern Roman Emperor, conducts a military campaign against Gothic tribes on the Danube frontier. The Goths, a Germanic people, have been pressing against the Roman frontier for decades. Valens' campaign is part of his effort to stabilize the Eastern empire's northern frontier. The campaign is partially successful, but Gothic pressure remains a persistent threat. This campaign foreshadows the later Gothic crisis that will culminate in the Battle of Adrianople (378 CE).
key_figures: Gothic tribal leaders (names not preserved in sources)
events.certainty_note: Ammianus Marcellinus documents Valens' Gothic campaigns, though the precise chronology of individual campaigns (365–378) is sometimes compressed or uncertain in the surviving text.
cross_references: Part of Valens' broader frontier stabilization strategy (364–378 CE)
Leads to Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)

events.title: Shapur II of Sasanid Persia campaigns in the Caucasus
events.description: Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE), Sasanid Shah, conducts military campaigns in the Caucasus region to consolidate Sasanid control and suppress local rebellions. The Caucasus is a strategically important region between the Sasanid and Roman empires, and control of the passes and kingdoms in the region is contested. Shapur's campaigns are part of his broader strategy to consolidate Sasanid power and manage the empire's vast territories.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (Roman perspective on Sasanid affairs)

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's reign and general activities are well attested. Specific campaigns in the Caucasus are less precisely dated in surviving sources; this entry reflects his general military activities during this period.
cross_references: Part of Shapur II's long reign and consolidation of Sasanid power (309–379 CE)
Contemporary with Roman frontier campaigns (368–375 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Valentinian I's Danube campaigns is sometimes overstated as decisive victories; modern scholarship emphasizes that these were part of ongoing, inconclusive struggles for frontier control. The frontier remained porous and contested throughout Valentinian's reign. Similarly, Valens' Gothic campaigns are sometimes portrayed as successful stabilization efforts, but they were ultimately inconclusive and did not prevent the Gothic crisis of 378 CE. The sources (Ammianus Marcellinus) sometimes compress multiple years of campaigning into a single narrative, making precise annual dating difficult.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 368 CE is moderately rich but heavily weighted toward Roman military and political narrative. Ammianus Marcellinus provides detailed accounts from a Roman perspective, but Germanic, Sarmatian, and Sasanid perspectives are largely absent or filtered through Roman accounts. Archaeological evidence for this period is sparse and regionally uneven. The surviving record privileges military history over economic, social, or cultural conditions. Eastern sources (Chinese, Indian) are largely absent from Western historical training data.

graph_edges.note: Part of Valentinian I's sustained frontier stabilization strategy (364–375 CE)
Valens' Gothic campaigns lead to the Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Part of Shapur II's long reign and consolidation of Sasanid power (309–379 CE)
